content.text: Community members raised serious concerns about the Eliza Foundation's financial arrangements. One post alleged that 750,000 CAD of holder assets were transferred into a company called SecureBlockchain Dev Corp during an April 2026 funding round, with a subsequent B2B development contract moving those same funds back to create the appearance of organic enterprise revenue. The post also noted that a co-founder of an acquired company called Agentric Solutions remains a defendant in an active legal case in the NY Southern District Court against Eliza. Separately, questions arose about large token movements from a wallet associated with Shaw that had been dormant for approximately eight months and moved from 1.02 billion tokens to zero. Community members acknowledged the legitimacy of these concerns and called for transparency.

Community members expressed ongoing frustration about the ElizaOS token, asking about staking and token utility features, questioning whether the token was still alive, and raising concerns about ongoing lawsuits and the lack of recent Twitter updates. One user noted that only Shaw can speak on token matters and that Shaw is currently too occupied with other priorities. Other members remained cautiously optimistic, with one noting that Shaw is active and working on Eliza without major technical blockers. A request was made for an official update from the team.

A new ElizaOS plugin called omniology/plugin-omniology was announced, enabling ElizaOS agents to autonomously compete in skill-based contests on Solana mainnet. The plugin enters agents into themed contests every 88 seconds covering categories such as Art, Story, and Joke, with an LLM judge scoring entries and paying winners in USDC on-chain. Early reported results included 289 contests, a 72 percent win rate, and 3.35 USDC earned. The entry fee is 0.01 USDC per contest.

Community members discussed the Ruby Trivia project and its $TRIVIA token, which launched on Solana as part of a multi-game trivia platform featuring server-scored games, live and async multiplayer, mobile support, and eight languages. Holding $TRIVIA provides an in-game score multiplier. An official Telegram channel was announced as live and a Twitter Space was announced as upcoming. One user repeatedly questioned whether the token was dead due to low chat and Twitter activity. Despite concerns, other members expressed optimism with one predicting the project could reach a 10 million valuation. A core developer acknowledged the need to post screenshots to demonstrate progress.

A user named Fixer posted an identical recruitment message in both the general discussion and coders channels, seeking an English-speaking client liaison for a development team expanding into the U.S. market. The post was met with skepticism and community moderators flagged the user along with another account as potential scammers. Separately, multiple developers posted availability notices throughout the week, including a Senior AI Full-Stack Software Engineer with expertise in React, Next.js, FastAPI, and agentic AI frameworks, and others offering development services via direct message.

A user named Nulucre dba Legacy Chain promoted a service called CallTyro in the coders channel, describing it as an open agent registry with reputation scoring and session contracts built for ElizaOS agents. According to the post, agents registered through CallTyro build a fulfillment rate and reputation score visible to other agents when deciding whether to connect. The user invited others to register for free at calltyro.com/register. Another user responded with a dismissive reaction.

ElizaOS completed a major architectural transition migrating core training, reinforcement learning, and local inference defaults from Qwen to Gemma 4 across all model sizes including E2B, E4B, 12B, and 31B variants. This migration ensures full compatibility with Eliza-1 release tiers and represents a foundational shift in the model ecosystem. Legacy Qwen configurations were replaced with Gemma turn-tokens across multiple pull requests, with the transition standardizing the entire framework across training, inference, and mobile platforms.

Cloud deployment infrastructure was significantly hardened across the week. Critical security measures deployed include IP-pinned safeFetch, environment variable denylists, isolated database provisioning, rate-limiting backstops, sybil-prevention guards, and a runtime kill-switch for cloud feature flags. A PR to isolate untrusted app builds from the runtime fleet was flagged as a high-priority Product-2 blocker. Network configuration was addressed by binding app host ports to loopback and routing through a node-local Caddy instance. Production-wide apps-deploy was enabled and critical bugs affecting cloud sign-in flows and agent conversation stability were resolved.

A robust agent handoff flow was implemented with message queuing to ensure zero data loss during agent switches, an invisible re-point mechanism to prevent UI flashes, automated cleanup of transient bridges, and end-to-end verification for handoff flows in the cloud test harness. Production regressions were resolved where agents were incorrectly defaulting to local inference instead of cloud topology. Full input coverage for macOS desktop computer use was achieved by resolving window listing and platform-specific bugs. The ecosystem was also expanded with the integration of plugin-x402-endpoints into the community registry, providing access to 28 global data endpoints.

Voice and vision pipelines were hardened by enhancing live half-duplex voice interactions through advanced acoustic echo cancellation primitives and activating the Kokoro TTS engine. Agent startup latency was reduced via telemetry-driven boot breakdowns and persistent warm hosts for vision and computer-use tasks. Codebase stability was improved by enforcing strict type-checking and refactoring Turbo build tasks for better cache efficiency. CI and build system optimizations included consolidation of model-provider build scripts and improved Turbo dependency management to decrease build times.

Earlier in the week, infrastructure improvements targeted core system stability. A fallback mechanism was introduced to the uncapped path for direct-reply requests to improve resilience in reply handling. Stale-job recovery sizing was adjusted to prevent provision flapping during cold-boot scenarios. Agent logic was updated to correctly handle lookups using the all: true parameter for pinned dual-stack web-fetch configurations, resolving IP-related errors. Streaming documentation was also finalized for token-by-token dashboard streaming with in-browser video evidence verifying functionality against Together AI cloud models.
